q: What is a Docker image layer, and why does layering matter?
a: An image is a stack of read-only layers, one per Dockerfile instruction (RUN, COPY, ADD). Each layer stores only the filesystem diff from the layer below, and layers are content-addressed so identical layers are shared across images and cached. At runtime Docker adds a thin writable layer on top via a union/overlay filesystem, leaving the image layers immutable and reusable.
fs: Layers are immutable content-addressed diffs unioned by overlayfs, which is why caching and cross-image dedup work.
fj: A layer is just a copy of the whole image you get after each command.

followups.q: If a file is created in one layer and deleted in a later layer, does that shrink the image?
followups.a: No. The earlier layer still contains the file's full bytes; the later layer only records a whiteout marker that hides it at runtime. The total image size still includes both, which is why secrets baked into an early layer leak via `docker history` and why cleanup must happen in the same RUN that created the data.

followups.q: Do all Dockerfile instructions create a layer?
followups.a: No. Only RUN, COPY, and ADD produce filesystem layers. Metadata-only instructions like ENV, LABEL, EXPOSE, CMD, ENTRYPOINT, and WORKDIR just update the image config and create zero-size layers, so they don't add to image size.

followups.q: How does the overlay filesystem present a single view from these stacked layers?
followups.a: `overlay2` mounts the read-only image layers as `lowerdir` and the container's writable layer as `upperdir`, merging them into one tree. Reads come from the topmost layer that has the file; a write to a lowerdir file triggers copy-up into the upperdir, where it's modified, leaving the underlying layer untouched.

q: How does the Docker build cache decide whether to reuse a layer?
a: For each instruction, BuildKit computes a cache key from the instruction text plus the inputs it touches. For COPY/ADD the key includes a checksum of the source files; for RUN it is the command string (it does not inspect what the command actually produced). If the key matches a previously built layer the cache is reused; the first miss invalidates every subsequent layer in the stage. That is why you order least-changing steps first.
fs: COPY is checksum-keyed but RUN is keyed only on the command string, so `apt-get update` alone can serve stale cache.
fj: Docker re-checks the actual file contents on every RUN to decide caching.

followups.q: Your CI runs on fresh runners every time, so the local cache is always cold. How do you still get cache hits?
followups.a: Use BuildKit's external cache backends: `--cache-to`/`--cache-from` (e.g. `type=registry` or `type=gha`) export layer cache to a registry or GitHub Actions cache, and the next runner imports it. With `mode=max` even intermediate stage layers are exported, so multi-stage builds get full cache reuse across ephemeral machines.

followups.q: How do you force a cache bust at a specific point without editing the actual command?
followups.a: Pass an `ARG` that you change to invalidate from that line down, e.g. `ARG CACHEBUST` then reference it before the RUN, building with `--build-arg CACHEBUST=$(date +%s)`. Alternatively `docker build --no-cache` rebuilds everything, or `--no-cache-filter=<stage>` busts a single stage.

followups.q: Why might a COPY layer miss the cache even when the file contents are identical?
followups.a: BuildKit's COPY checksum includes file metadata like mode and ownership, not just bytes, so a permission change busts it. Also, anything earlier in the stage missing cache cascades down. With the classic (non-BuildKit) builder, even unrelated mtime changes could invalidate COPY since it was less precise about what it hashed.

q: Why put COPY of dependency manifests before COPY of source code?
a: The first cache miss invalidates all later layers, so you isolate the slow, rarely-changing step (installing dependencies) above the fast, frequently-changing step (your source). Copying only package.json/lockfile and running the install first means edits to application code do not bust the dependency layer. This can turn a multi-minute rebuild into a few seconds.
fs: Order layers by change frequency: a code edit should never invalidate the dependency-install layer.
fj: Just `COPY . .` first; it is simpler and the cache still works fine.

followups.q: In a monorepo with many package.json files, copying just the root manifest isn't enough. How do you preserve the dependency-layer cache?
followups.a: Copy every manifest and lockfile across all workspaces before the install, but nothing else — e.g. `COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./` plus each `packages/*/package.json`. The install layer then only busts when a dependency actually changes. BuildKit cache mounts on the package-manager store further speed warm installs.

followups.q: What's the failure mode if you copy the lockfile but install with a command that ignores it?
followups.a: You lose reproducibility: `npm install` may resolve newer versions than the lockfile pins, so the built image drifts from what was tested even though the cache layer looks stable. Use `npm ci` (or `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`), which installs exactly the lockfile and fails if it's out of sync.

followups.q: Does this ordering still help if your dependencies themselves change on most commits?
followups.a: Less so — if the lockfile changes every build, the install layer busts every build regardless of ordering, and you gain little. In that case the win shifts to a cache mount that persists the download cache across builds, so even an invalidated install layer re-fetches little from the network.

q: What is a multi-stage build and what problem does it solve?
a: A multi-stage Dockerfile defines several FROM stages; you build in a stage that has compilers and dev tools, then COPY --from only the finished artifacts into a clean, minimal final stage. The final image inherits nothing from earlier stages except what you explicitly copy, so build-time tooling, caches, and secrets never ship. It is the standard way to keep a small, secure runtime image without a separate build script.
fs: COPY --from leaves toolchains and build secrets behind in the builder stage, so only artifacts reach the runtime image.
fj: Multi-stage just means writing multiple Dockerfiles and merging them.

followups.q: Can you build or stop at just one stage without producing the whole image?
followups.a: Yes. `docker build --target build .` stops at the named stage, which is handy for producing a test or lint image, or pulling artifacts out without the final stage. BuildKit also skips any stage whose output no target depends on, so unused stages cost nothing.

followups.q: Beyond copying from a previous FROM stage, what else can COPY --from reference?
followups.a: It can copy directly from an external image, e.g. `COPY --from=ghcr.io/org/tools:1.2 /usr/bin/tool /usr/bin/tool`, with no need to declare it as a stage. This is a common way to grab a single static binary (like a CLI) from a published image into your runtime stage.

followups.q: You're building a CGO-enabled Go binary and the distroless/static runtime crashes with a missing shared library. Why?
followups.a: `distroless/static` has no glibc, so a dynamically-linked (CGO) binary can't resolve `libc.so`. Either build a fully static binary (`CGO_ENABLED=0`) to keep using `static`, or switch the runtime to `gcr.io/distroless/base` (or `cc`), which ship the C runtime your binary links against.

q: Your final image is 1.2 GB. Walk through how you would slim it.
a: Start from a smaller base (slim/alpine/distroless) and use a multi-stage build so compilers and caches stay in the builder. Combine RUN steps and clean package caches in the same layer (a separate cleanup RUN does not shrink earlier layers), use a .dockerignore to keep build context out, and copy only needed artifacts rather than the whole builder. Then measure with `docker history` or dive to find the fat layers.
fs: Cleanup must happen in the same RUN that created the bloat, because a later RUN rm only adds a layer on top.
fj: Add `RUN rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` as a final layer to shrink the image.

followups.q: A teammate suggests just switching the base to Alpine to fix size. What can go wrong?
followups.a: Alpine uses musl libc, not glibc, so glibc-linked binaries, some Python wheels (manylinux), and certain native modules break or fall back to slow source builds. You may chase DNS-resolution and locale quirks too. Distroless or `-slim` glibc bases often give most of the size win without the musl footguns.

followups.q: You've already optimized the Dockerfile but one layer is still huge. How do you pinpoint exactly what's in it?
followups.a: `docker history --no-trunc <image>` shows per-layer size and the instruction that created it. For a file-level view, `dive` lets you walk each layer's filesystem and flags wasted space (files added then deleted in later layers), so you can see precisely which paths to move or clean in-layer.

followups.q: Squashing layers is sometimes proposed to shrink images. Why is it usually a bad trade?
followups.a: Squashing (`--squash` or exporting flattened) collapses all layers into one, which can drop intermediate bloat but destroys layer sharing — every image now ships a unique blob, so pulls and registry storage balloon across many images. Prefer fixing the layers (in-layer cleanup, multi-stage) so the common base layers stay deduplicated.

q: What is a distroless image and what is the tradeoff?
a: Distroless images contain only your app and its runtime dependencies, with no package manager and no shell. That shrinks attack surface and size and forces non-root by default with the :nonroot tag. The tradeoff is debuggability: you cannot `docker exec` a shell, so you rely on ephemeral debug containers, app-level logging, and exec-form healthchecks instead of shell scripts.
fs: No shell means no `exec sh` to debug; you attach an ephemeral debug container via `docker debug`/`kubectl debug` instead.
fj: Distroless is just Alpine with a smaller package list.

followups.q: Your HEALTHCHECK uses `CMD curl -f ...` but the distroless image has no curl or shell. How do you health-check it?
followups.a: Ship a tiny static health-probe binary alongside your app and call it in exec form, e.g. `HEALTHCHECK CMD ["/app/healthcheck"]`. Go's `grpc_health_probe` or a few-line static binary is common. In Kubernetes, prefer the platform's `httpGet`/`grpc` probes, which don't need any in-image tooling at all.

followups.q: What does the `:debug` variant of a distroless image give you, and why not use it everywhere?
followups.a: The `:debug` tag adds a BusyBox shell and basic tools so you can `exec` in to troubleshoot. You don't run it in production because it re-introduces a shell and utilities — extra attack surface — defeating the point. Use it as a temporary swap or via an ephemeral debug container, then revert.

followups.q: How does an ephemeral debug container let you inspect a distroless container that has no tools?
followups.a: `kubectl debug` (or `docker debug`) starts a separate container with your chosen toolbox image and joins it to the target's namespaces (PID, network, sometimes filesystem via `--target`). You get your tools' processes seeing the target's process tree and network, without ever adding anything to the production image.

q: What does .dockerignore do and why does it matter for builds?
a: The .dockerignore file excludes paths from the build context that the CLI sends to the daemon before the build starts. Excluding things like .git, node_modules, and local secrets makes the context smaller (faster uploads), prevents busting COPY cache on irrelevant file changes, and avoids accidentally baking credentials into an image. It is evaluated against the context root, not against COPY destinations.
fs: It trims the context the daemon receives, so it both speeds uploads and stops secrets from leaking into layers.
fj: It is like .gitignore and only affects which files Docker tracks in version control.

followups.q: You .dockerignore'd `.env` but still need one specific file under an ignored directory. How do you do that?
followups.a: `.dockerignore` supports negation with `!`, so you ignore broadly then re-include: `secrets/*` followed by `!secrets/ca.pem`. Order matters — later patterns override earlier ones. Note you still shouldn't COPY real secrets in; use a BuildKit secret mount for those.

followups.q: With BuildKit and named contexts, does .dockerignore still apply the same way?
followups.a: Each context has its own ignore file. For the default context it's `.dockerignore`; BuildKit also honors a per-Dockerfile `<dockerfile-name>.dockerignore` if present, which takes precedence. For additional named contexts (`--build-context`) the relevant ignore rules apply to that context's root, not the main one.

followups.q: If a path is excluded by .dockerignore but a COPY tries to reference it, what happens?
followups.a: The build fails or the file is simply absent, because the excluded path was never sent to the builder — it's not in the context at all. This is a common source of "file not found" COPY errors after someone tightens the ignore file; the fix is a `!` negation to re-include the needed path.

q: Explain ENTRYPOINT vs CMD and when you would use each.
a: ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed executable that always runs; CMD provides default arguments (or a default command) that are easy to override at `docker run`. Used together, ENTRYPOINT ["app"] plus CMD ["--help"] makes the container behave like the `app` binary whose default args you can swap. Use ENTRYPOINT to lock the program and CMD for the parts callers should be able to change.
fs: ENTRYPOINT fixes the binary, CMD supplies overridable default args; run args replace CMD, --entrypoint replaces ENTRYPOINT.
fj: They are interchangeable, so use whichever you remember.

followups.q: What happens if ENTRYPOINT is in exec form but CMD is in shell form (or vice versa)?
followups.a: When ENTRYPOINT is exec form and CMD is shell form, Docker appends `/bin/sh -c "<cmd>"` as the arguments — usually not what you want. The reliable pattern is to keep both in exec (JSON) form so CMD's tokens are appended cleanly as argv to the ENTRYPOINT binary.

followups.q: How does an entrypoint script let you do setup before handing off to the real process correctly?
followups.a: Write `entrypoint.sh` that does init work, then `exec "$@"` to replace the shell with the command (CMD) as PID 1 so it receives signals. Without `exec`, the shell stays PID 1 and swallows SIGTERM. This is the standard pattern for running migrations or fixing permissions before the app starts.

followups.q: A user wants to run a one-off shell in your image that has an ENTRYPOINT. What's the gotcha?
followups.a: Run args only replace CMD, not ENTRYPOINT, so `docker run img sh` becomes `entrypoint sh` — likely an error. You must override the entrypoint: `docker run -it --entrypoint sh img`. This is exactly why a hard-coded ENTRYPOINT can frustrate debugging if it's not designed to pass through.

q: Why prefer the exec form (JSON array) over the shell form for ENTRYPOINT/CMD?
a: Shell form (ENTRYPOINT cmd arg) runs your command under /bin/sh -c, so the shell becomes PID 1 and your process is its child. That shell typically does not forward SIGTERM, so `docker stop` cannot gracefully shut your app down and you get a hard SIGKILL after the timeout. Exec form (ENTRYPOINT ["cmd","arg"]) runs the binary directly as PID 1 so it receives signals, but loses shell features like variable expansion.
fs: Shell form makes /bin/sh PID 1, which swallows SIGTERM and breaks graceful shutdown on docker stop.
fj: Use shell form so you can use $ENV variables; the form does not affect shutdown.

followups.q: How long does docker stop wait before the SIGKILL, and how do you change it?
followups.a: By default Docker sends SIGTERM (or the image's STOPSIGNAL) then waits a 10-second grace period before SIGKILL. Override per call with `docker stop -t 30`, or set a longer default; `-t -1` waits indefinitely. Long-draining services need a timeout above their worst-case shutdown so they aren't killed mid-flush.

followups.q: You need shell features like env expansion but still want correct signal handling. How?
followups.a: Wrap with exec form invoking the shell explicitly and use `exec` to hand off: `ENTRYPOINT ["sh", "-c", "exec myapp --port $PORT"]`. The `exec` replaces the shell process with the app as PID 1, so you get variable expansion at start and proper SIGTERM delivery afterward.

followups.q: Even with exec form, your app is PID 1 and leaves zombie processes. What's the fix?
followups.a: PID 1 has special duties — it must reap orphaned children — and many apps don't. Run a tiny init like `tini` (or `docker run --init`) as PID 1; it reaps zombies and forwards signals to your app. This is why `--init` exists as a one-flag fix for both reaping and signal forwarding.

q: A container exits immediately on `docker run`. How do you debug it?
a: First check `docker ps -a` for the exit code and `docker logs <id>` for stderr; a nonzero code usually means the entrypoint crashed. Inspect the configured command with `docker inspect` and confirm the binary path and that the process stays in the foreground (a backgrounded process makes PID 1 exit). If it is a startup config issue, override the entrypoint to drop into a shell: `docker run -it --entrypoint sh image`.
fs: Override the entrypoint to a shell and re-run interactively to reproduce the crash with the real image filesystem.
fj: Just rebuild the image; if it exits the image must be corrupted.

followups.q: The container exits 0 immediately, not with an error. What's typically happening?
followups.a: The main process finished and there's nothing to keep PID 1 alive — common when the app daemonizes/backgrounds itself, or the command is a one-shot. Containers live only as long as PID 1. Run the server in the foreground (e.g. `nginx -g 'daemon off;'`) so the process the container watches doesn't exit.

followups.q: What does exit code 137 tell you specifically?
followups.a: 137 = 128 + 9, meaning the process was killed by signal 9 (SIGKILL). Most often that's an OOM kill (the kernel reaped it for exceeding the memory cgroup limit) or a `docker stop` that timed out. Check `docker inspect`'s `OOMKilled` field and raise `--memory` or fix the leak accordingly.

followups.q: Logs are empty and the exit is instant. How else can you see why it died?
followups.a: `docker inspect <id>` shows `State.ExitCode`, `Error`, and `OOMKilled`. If it's a missing-binary or bad-interpreter case you'll see an exec error like "no such file or directory" (often a wrong arch image or a CRLF in the entrypoint script). Re-running with `--entrypoint sh -it` lets you probe the filesystem and run the command manually.

q: How do you debug a running container that is misbehaving?
a: Use `docker exec -it <id> sh` to get a shell inside its namespaces and inspect processes, env, and files; `docker logs -f` to stream stdout/stderr; and `docker stats`/`docker top` for resource and process views. For images without a shell (distroless/scratch) use `docker debug` or `kubectl debug` to attach an ephemeral debug container that shares the target's namespaces. Use `docker inspect` for config, mounts, and network details.
fs: For shell-less images, attach an ephemeral debug container sharing the target's PID/network namespace instead of exec.
fj: SSH into the container like a VM and look around.

followups.q: Why does `docker exec` see a different process tree and environment than the container's main process?
followups.a: `exec` starts a brand-new process that joins the container's namespaces but does NOT inherit the entrypoint's environment — it gets the image's ENV plus any `--env` you pass, not variables the app set at runtime. It shares the PID namespace, so `ps` shows the app, but env vars exported inside the original process won't appear.

followups.q: You suspect a network issue but the app's image has no networking tools. How do you inspect its traffic?
followups.a: Run a tool container in the target's network namespace: `docker run --net container:<id> nicolaka/netshoot` gives you `tcpdump`, `dig`, `ss`, and `curl` seeing exactly the target's interfaces and DNS. Nothing is added to the app image, and you can capture packets or test resolution as if you were inside it.

followups.q: How do you grab a CPU or memory profile from a container without restarting it?
followups.a: Exec the language's profiler or expose a profiling endpoint (e.g. Go `pprof`, JVM async-profiler) and port-forward to it. For a quick view, `docker stats` shows live CPU/mem, and `docker exec` lets you run `top`/`perf` inside. The key is the app must already expose the hooks; you can't bolt on a profiler the binary doesn't support.

q: What is the difference between a Docker volume and a bind mount?
a: A named volume is managed by Docker under its own directory (e.g. /var/lib/docker/volumes), is portable across hosts via drivers, and is the recommended way to persist app data. A bind mount maps an arbitrary host path straight into the container, giving the container the host's exact files and permissions; it is great for live-editing source in development but couples you to the host layout. Both outlive the container; an anonymous volume has a random name and is easy to orphan.
fs: Volumes are Docker-managed and portable; bind mounts expose a host path verbatim and are dev/host-coupled.
fj: They are the same thing; a volume is just a folder on the host you pick.

followups.q: When you mount a named volume onto a path that already has files in the image, what happens to those files?
followups.a: An empty named volume mounted over a populated image directory gets pre-seeded with the image's contents on first use — Docker copies the existing files into the new volume. A bind mount does NOT do this; it shadows the directory entirely, so the image's files at that path become invisible behind the host directory.

followups.q: Why might a bind-mounted node_modules cause native-module crashes across host and container?
followups.a: Bind mounts expose the host's exact bytes, so modules compiled against the host's OS/arch (e.g. macOS) get loaded by a Linux container and fail. The common fix is an anonymous volume on `/app/node_modules` that masks the host copy, letting the container install its own platform-correct binaries.

followups.q: How do you safely back up the data in a named volume?
followups.a: Run a throwaway container that mounts both the volume and a host backup dir, then tar it out: `docker run --rm -v pgdata:/data -v $PWD:/backup alpine tar czf /backup/pgdata.tgz -C /data .`. For databases specifically, prefer a logical dump (`pg_dump`) over a raw volume copy to avoid an inconsistent on-disk snapshot.

q: Why do containers on a custom bridge network resolve each other by name, but not on the default bridge?
a: User-defined bridge networks enable Docker's embedded DNS server at 127.0.0.11, which resolves container names and network aliases to their current IPs automatically. The default `bridge` network does not provide this DNS-based service discovery, so containers there can only reach each other by IP (the legacy --link aside). That is why best practice is to create a user-defined network and let services talk by name.
fs: The embedded DNS at 127.0.0.11 only runs on user-defined networks, so name resolution is a reason to never use default bridge.
fj: Container names always resolve as hostnames, regardless of which network you use.

followups.q: Two containers run the same service and you want one DNS name to load-balance across them. How?
followups.a: Give both the same `--network-alias` on a user-defined network; the embedded DNS returns multiple A records (round-robin) for that alias. In Swarm, a service VIP fronts the tasks instead. This is how Compose `scale`d replicas become reachable under one service name.

followups.q: A container is attached to two networks. Which network's DNS and gateway does it use?
followups.a: It gets an interface and IP on each network and can resolve names on any network it's attached to via the embedded DNS. Outbound default-route traffic uses the gateway of the network with the lowest-priority/default interface (Docker picks one as default), so attaching to multiple networks is how you bridge, e.g., a frontend net and a backend net.

followups.q: Why can't the container just edit /etc/resolv.conf to point elsewhere, and where does upstream DNS come from?
followups.a: On a user-defined network, `/etc/resolv.conf` points at `127.0.0.11`, the embedded DNS. For names it doesn't know (external domains), it forwards to the daemon's configured upstream resolvers (the host's resolvers by default, or `--dns` flags). Overwriting resolv.conf inside the container breaks Docker's service discovery, so you set DNS via run flags instead.

q: What are Docker's main network drivers and when do you use each?
a: bridge is the default single-host driver, giving containers a private subnet behind NAT; user-defined bridges add DNS service discovery. host shares the host's network namespace with no isolation or port mapping, used for max performance. none disables networking. overlay spans multiple hosts (Swarm/multi-host) and macvlan gives a container its own MAC/IP on the physical LAN.
fs: host removes the network namespace entirely, so there is no port publishing and the container binds host ports directly.
fj: You always use bridge; the other drivers are basically never needed.

followups.q: What's the catch with host networking on Docker Desktop (Mac/Windows)?
followups.a: On Mac/Windows, Docker runs in a Linux VM, so `--network host` historically didn't map to the actual host — it bound inside the VM. Docker Desktop added opt-in host networking support, but it's not the seamless host-equals-laptop behavior Linux gives. On Linux native, host networking truly shares the host stack.

followups.q: Why does macvlan often fail to let the container talk to its own host, and what's the workaround?
followups.a: macvlan attaches the container to the physical NIC at L2, but most NICs won't loop a frame from a macvlan child back to the parent host — so host↔container traffic is blocked by design. The workaround is creating a second macvlan/ipvlan sub-interface on the host for that path, or just using a bridge network for host-local comms.

followups.q: When would you reach for an overlay network outside of Docker Swarm?
followups.a: Overlay needs a key-value store / control plane to coordinate the VXLAN tunnels and IP allocation across hosts; Swarm provides that out of the box. Outside Swarm you'd point the overlay driver at an external KV store (legacy) — but in practice multi-host networking today is handled by Kubernetes CNI plugins, not standalone Docker overlay.

q: What does publishing a port (-p 8080:80) actually do?
a: It tells Docker to map a host port to a container port by programming an iptables DNAT rule (via the userland proxy/NAT) so traffic to the host port is forwarded into the container's network namespace. Without publishing, a container on a bridge network is only reachable from other containers on that network, not from the host or outside. The left side is the host port, the right side is the in-container port.
fs: -p installs a DNAT/iptables rule; EXPOSE only documents intent and publishes nothing by itself.
fj: EXPOSE in the Dockerfile already opens the port to the host.

followups.q: You published a port but the container still isn't reachable from outside. What do you check?
followups.a: Confirm the app binds `0.0.0.0`, not `127.0.0.1` — a loopback bind inside the container is unreachable through the DNAT. Also check `docker port <id>` for the actual mapping, host firewall rules, and that you didn't bind to a specific host IP that differs from how you're connecting (`-p 127.0.0.1:8080:80` only listens on localhost).

followups.q: Why might Docker's published ports bypass your UFW/host firewall rules?
followups.a: Docker inserts its DNAT rules into iptables `nat`/`DOCKER` chains ahead of many user firewall rules, so a published port can be open to the world even though UFW "denies" it. The fix is to bind to a specific interface (`-p 127.0.0.1:...`) for local-only, or configure `DOCKER-USER` chain rules / `iptables=false` deliberately.

followups.q: What is the userland proxy (docker-proxy) and when can you turn it off?
followups.a: `docker-proxy` is a small process that forwards published-port traffic for cases iptables DNAT alone can't cover (e.g. host-loopback to a published port, or hairpin). It adds a little overhead and a process per mapping. With `userland-proxy: false` in the daemon config, Docker relies purely on iptables/NAT, which is fine on modern kernels for most workloads.

q: What is Docker Compose and what does a service definition give you?
a: Compose declares a multi-container app in a YAML file and brings it up with one command (`docker compose up`). Each service is a container spec (image, ports, env, volumes, depends_on); Compose creates a shared user-defined network so services resolve each other by service name, and named volumes for persistence. It is meant for local dev and single-host orchestration, not as a cluster scheduler.
fs: Compose auto-creates a project network so services reach each other by service name, no manual --link or IP wiring.
fj: Compose is a production cluster orchestrator that replaces Kubernetes.

followups.q: How do multiple Compose files or an override file combine, and why is that useful?
followups.a: Compose merges `compose.yaml` with `compose.override.yaml` (auto-loaded) or any files passed with `-f`, deep-merging maps and replacing scalars in later files. This lets a base file describe the app and an override add dev-only bind mounts or a prod file swap images, so you keep one source of truth per environment.

followups.q: What's the difference between `docker compose up`, `down`, and `down -v` for your data?
followups.a: `up` creates/starts; `down` stops and removes containers and the project network but KEEPS named volumes by default; `down -v` also deletes named volumes — destroying your database. That `-v` is the classic foot-gun: people run it to "clean up" and wipe persistent data. `stop` just halts containers without removing anything.

followups.q: How do you scale a service to multiple replicas in Compose, and what breaks?
followups.a: `docker compose up --scale web=3` runs three containers behind the service-name DNS alias (round-robined). It breaks if you statically published a host port (`8080:80`), since three containers can't bind the same host port — use a range or front them with a load-balancer/reverse proxy that targets the service name.

q: Does Compose `depends_on` guarantee the dependency is ready before the next service starts?
a: No. Plain depends_on only controls start order, not readiness; it starts the dependency's container first but does not wait for the app inside to accept connections. To actually wait for readiness, give the dependency a healthcheck and use `depends_on: { db: { condition: service_healthy } }`. Apps should still retry connections, because nothing fully guarantees availability.
fs: depends_on orders container starts, not readiness; you need condition: service_healthy plus a healthcheck to wait for ready.
fj: depends_on waits until the database is fully up and accepting queries.

followups.q: Even with condition: service_healthy, why should the app still retry its DB connection?
followups.a: Health is a point-in-time signal and the DB can blip after startup — failover, a restart, or a transient network drop — long after the dependency was "healthy" at boot. Connection retry with backoff makes the app resilient to mid-life outages, not just the cold-start race that depends_on addresses.

followups.q: What does `condition: service_completed_successfully` enable, and when is it used?
followups.a: It makes a service wait until another service's container exits 0 — ideal for a one-shot init/migration job: the `migrate` service runs to completion, then `web` starts. If the job fails (nonzero exit), the dependent service won't start, so a bad migration blocks the rollout cleanly.

followups.q: On a Swarm deployment, does depends_on still apply?
followups.a: No — `depends_on` is ignored by `docker stack deploy` (Swarm) entirely. Swarm assumes services come up in any order and converge, so you must build retry/health logic into the apps themselves. depends_on ordering is a Compose-local-dev feature, not an orchestration guarantee.

q: What does the HEALTHCHECK instruction do and what are its key options?
a: HEALTHCHECK defines a command Docker runs periodically to report a container as healthy or unhealthy, which orchestrators and `depends_on: service_healthy` can act on. Key options are --interval (time between checks), --timeout (per-check deadline), --retries (consecutive failures before unhealthy), and --start-period (a grace window where failures don't count, for slow boots). The probe must exit 0 for healthy or 1 for unhealthy.
fs: --start-period gives a grace window where failing probes don't count, so slow JVM/DB warmups aren't killed prematurely.
fj: A healthcheck automatically restarts the container when the app crashes.

followups.q: A container is marked unhealthy but keeps running. What actually acts on the health status?
followups.a: Plain Docker does nothing but set the status — it won't restart an unhealthy container on its own. An orchestrator does: Swarm reschedules unhealthy tasks, and `--restart` combined with tooling, or Compose `depends_on: service_healthy`, reacts to it. To auto-restart on health failure you need Swarm or an external watcher; HEALTHCHECK alone just reports.

followups.q: Your healthcheck command does heavy work every interval and shows up as load. How do you keep it cheap?
followups.a: Make the probe a lightweight liveness endpoint that doesn't hit the database or do real work, lengthen `--interval`, and keep `--timeout` tight. A health probe that runs a full query every few seconds can itself cause load spikes or false unhealthies under pressure, so it should be near-free to evaluate.

followups.q: How do liveness vs readiness differ, and does Docker's HEALTHCHECK cover both?
followups.a: Liveness = "is the process alive, restart if not"; readiness = "can it serve traffic right now, route around it if not." Docker's HEALTHCHECK is a single signal that conflates them. Kubernetes splits them into separate `livenessProbe` and `readinessProbe`, which is why on K8s you generally use those instead of the image's HEALTHCHECK.

q: Why run a container as a non-root user, and how do you do it?
a: By default a container's PID 1 runs as root, and although it is namespaced, a kernel or misconfiguration escape then has root on the host, plus root can write anywhere in mounts. Add a dedicated user/group in the Dockerfile, give it ownership of needed paths, and switch with USER so the app drops privileges. Combine with --cap-drop, read-only root filesystem, and no-new-privileges to harden further.
fs: USER drops privileges at runtime; pair it with cap-drop ALL, read-only rootfs, and no-new-privileges for defense in depth.
fj: Containers are already isolated, so running as root inside is perfectly safe.

followups.q: After switching to USER app, the container can't write its log/cache directory. Why and how do you fix it?
followups.a: Files created by earlier root-owned layers are owned by root, so the non-root user can't write them. Fix it at build time by chowning the needed paths (`RUN chown -R app:app /data`) or `COPY --chown=app:app`, and mount writable dirs as volumes. With a read-only rootfs, give writable paths a `tmpfs` or named volume.

followups.q: Your non-root app needs to bind port 80. How do you allow that without running as root?
followups.a: Either grant the binary the `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` capability (`setcap` at build, or `--cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE`), or just bind a high port (8080) inside and publish it as `-p 80:8080`. Since Docker 20.10 the daemon also lets you lower the unprivileged-port floor, but publishing a high in-container port is the simplest, safest route.

followups.q: How does Docker's user namespace remapping differ from just setting USER in the Dockerfile?
followups.a: USER changes the in-container UID but, without remapping, container root still equals host root (UID 0). `userns-remap` maps the container's UID range to an unprivileged host range, so even container root is a powerless user on the host — defense if something runs as root inside. They're complementary: USER for least privilege in-app, userns-remap for host-side isolation.

q: How should secrets be passed into images and containers, and what is the wrong way?
a: Do not bake secrets via ENV or ARG or COPY: ARG values and prior layers remain visible in image history even if a later layer deletes them. At build time use BuildKit secret mounts (RUN --mount=type=secret) so the value is available to a command without being written to a layer. At runtime inject via env files, Docker/Swarm secrets, or an external secrets manager, and keep them out of the image entirely.
fs: ARG/ENV secrets persist in image history and layers; use --mount=type=secret at build and a secrets manager at runtime.
fj: Pass the secret with ARG and delete it in a later RUN so it's gone.

followups.q: Where exactly does a BuildKit secret mount expose the value, and is it in the final image?
followups.a: By default the secret is mounted as a file at `/run/secrets/<id>` only for the duration of that single RUN, on a tmpfs. It's never written to a layer or the build cache key, so it doesn't appear in `docker history` or the final image. You pass it with `docker build --secret id=npm,src=./token`.

followups.q: Passing a secret as a runtime env var is common but has a leak vector. What is it?
followups.a: Environment variables are visible to anyone who can `docker inspect` the container, are inherited by child processes, and often get dumped into logs or crash reports. They're better than baking into the image, but for sensitive material a mounted file (Docker/Swarm secret at `/run/secrets`) or a secrets-manager fetch at startup is safer than `-e`.

followups.q: How do Docker Swarm secrets get delivered to a service, and why is that better than env?
followups.a: Swarm stores the secret encrypted in the Raft log and mounts it into the task as an in-memory tmpfs file at `/run/secrets/<name>`, only on nodes running that service. It's never written to disk on the worker and not exposed via inspect of the container, so it avoids the env-var and image-layer leak paths entirely.

q: What is the difference between an image and a container?
a: An image is an immutable, read-only template: a stack of layers plus metadata (entrypoint, env, exposed ports). A container is a running (or stopped) instance of an image with a thin writable layer on top and its own process, network, and mount namespaces. You can start many containers from one image, and changes inside a container stay in its writable layer unless you commit or persist them to a volume.
fs: Container = image layers + a writable layer + namespaces/cgroups; the image stays immutable and shareable across instances.
fj: A container is just the image after you start it; there's no real difference.

followups.q: What happens to a container's writable layer when you stop versus remove the container?
followups.a: Stopping keeps the writable layer, so you can `docker start` and your in-container changes are still there. `docker rm` deletes that writable layer permanently — any data not in a volume is gone. This is why ephemeral state survives a restart but not a recreate, and why durable data must live in a volume.

followups.q: What does docker commit do, and why is it discouraged for building images?
followups.a: `docker commit` snapshots a container's writable layer into a new image. It's discouraged because the result is opaque and non-reproducible — there's no Dockerfile recording how it was made, so it can't be rebuilt, reviewed, or rebased on a patched base. Use it only for ad-hoc capture; build real images from a Dockerfile.

followups.q: Multiple containers run from the same image. Do they share the underlying layer storage?
followups.a: Yes — the read-only image layers are shared on disk via the storage driver; each container only adds its own thin writable layer. So ten containers of the same image cost one copy of the image plus ten small writable layers, which is a big reason container density is cheap compared to full VMs.

q: Why does `apt-get update` in one RUN and `apt-get install` in a later RUN cause stale-package bugs?
a: Each RUN is a cached layer keyed on its command string, not its output. If the install layer is cached from a previous build, Docker reuses it even though a fresh `update` layer fetched newer package indexes, so you install against stale metadata or miss the new index entirely. Always chain `apt-get update && apt-get install` in a single RUN so they cache and invalidate together.
fs: Splitting update/install across RUN layers lets the install reuse stale cache; chain them in one RUN so they invalidate atomically.
fj: Separate RUN lines are cleaner and the result is identical to chaining them.

followups.q: Why is `--no-install-recommends` recommended alongside the install?
followups.a: apt pulls "Recommends" packages by default, which adds docs, optional tools, and extra libraries you rarely need, bloating the image and attack surface. `--no-install-recommends` installs only hard dependencies, often cutting tens to hundreds of MB. You add specific extras explicitly when actually required.

followups.q: If you want a BuildKit cache mount for apt to speed rebuilds, what extra flag is essential and why?
followups.a: Use `sharing=locked` on the cache mount: `RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/var/cache/apt,sharing=locked`. apt isn't safe under concurrent writers, so two parallel builds sharing the cache can corrupt it; `locked` serializes them. You also stop deleting `/var/cache/apt` since the mount, not a layer, holds it. Disable apt's auto-clean docker config so files persist in the cache.

followups.q: How do you pin package versions in apt so the build is reproducible?
followups.a: Use `apt-get install pkg=1.2.3-1` to lock exact versions, and pin the base image by digest so the apt repo snapshot is stable. The catch: distro repos garbage-collect old versions, so a pin can stop resolving over time — teams often run a frozen snapshot mirror (e.g. snapshot.debian.org) for truly long-lived reproducibility.

q: What is the build context and how does the daemon use it?
a: The build context is the set of files (the directory you point `docker build` at, or a Git URL/tarball) that the CLI packages and sends to the builder before the build runs. COPY and ADD can only reference paths inside that context, never arbitrary paths on your machine. A bloated context (sending node_modules or .git) slows every build because the whole bundle transfers first, even if no COPY touches those files. Trim it with .dockerignore so only what you actually copy is sent.
fs: COPY/ADD can only read files inside the context that was sent to the builder, not arbitrary host paths.
fj: The build context is just the current folder, and Docker only reads the files you COPY.

followups.q: Why does sending the context to a remote/daemon builder matter even when no COPY uses most files?
followups.a: The CLI transfers the whole context bundle to the builder before any instruction runs; with a remote daemon or a CI builder that's a network upload of every non-ignored byte. So a 2 GB context with node_modules adds upload latency to every build regardless of which files COPY references — which is exactly what .dockerignore prevents.

followups.q: How does BuildKit reduce the cost of large contexts compared to the legacy builder?
followups.a: BuildKit transfers the context incrementally and lazily — it sends only the files an instruction actually needs and caches the transfer, rather than tarring and shipping the entire directory upfront like the classic builder. It still respects .dockerignore, but the per-build penalty for a big untouched context is much smaller.

followups.q: Can you build with no local context at all, e.g. straight from a Git repo?
followups.a: Yes. `docker build https://github.com/org/repo.git#branch` makes the remote repo the context, and `docker build - < Dockerfile` builds with an empty context (no COPY of local files possible). BuildKit also supports `--build-context name=...` to add extra named contexts (dirs, images, or git URLs) referenced via `COPY --from=name`.

q: What is the difference between ADD and COPY, and which should you default to?
a: COPY does one thing: copy files and directories from the build context into the image. ADD does that plus two surprises — it can fetch a remote URL and it auto-extracts a local tar archive into the destination. Those extra behaviors are easy to trigger by accident and make builds harder to reason about, so the convention is to default to COPY and reach for ADD only when you specifically want tar auto-extraction (or use a checksum-verified remote fetch). Prefer an explicit RUN curl for downloads so you control verification.
fs: ADD auto-extracts local tarballs and can fetch URLs; COPY just copies, so default to COPY for predictability.
fj: ADD and COPY are the same; ADD is just the older spelling.

followups.q: Modern BuildKit added flags that make ADD safer for remote fetches. What are they?
followups.a: `ADD --checksum=sha256:... <url> <dest>` verifies the download's hash so a changed/poisoned remote fails the build, and `ADD --keep-git-dir` controls git-context behavior. With `--checksum` you get an integrity guarantee similar to the explicit curl-and-verify pattern, removing the main reason to avoid ADD for URLs.

followups.q: Why is `ADD <url>` for downloads still often discouraged even with checksums?
followups.a: ADD's URL fetch creates a layer that's re-evaluated on cache rules tied to the URL, doesn't let you decompress/clean in the same step, and you can't run intermediate verification logic. An explicit `RUN curl ... && verify && cleanup` keeps the download, check, and removal of temp files in one layer, which is leaner and more auditable.

followups.q: Does COPY --chown work on all base images?
followups.a: `COPY --chown=user:group` needs the user/group to exist (by name) in the image, or you can pass numeric UID:GID which always works even on `scratch`/distroless that lack `/etc/passwd`. On Windows containers `--chown` is unsupported. So for shell-less bases, prefer numeric IDs to avoid a name-lookup failure.

q: Explain the difference between ARG and ENV in a Dockerfile.
a: ARG declares a build-time variable you can pass with --build-arg; it exists only during the build and is not present in the running container. ENV sets a variable that is baked into the image and visible to every container started from it. ARG values are not secret — they persist in image history and build provenance — so never pass credentials as ARG. Each FROM starts a fresh scope, so an ARG must be redeclared in each stage that uses it; a common pattern is ARG VERSION then ENV APP_VERSION=$VERSION to promote a build value into runtime.
fs: ARG is build-only and absent at runtime; ENV is baked into the image and seen by every container.
fj: ARG and ENV both set environment variables the container can read at runtime.

followups.q: How do you use an ARG to parameterize the base image in FROM, and what's the scoping catch?
followups.a: An ARG declared before the first FROM (e.g. `ARG TAG=3.12`, then `FROM python:${TAG}`) is in a special global scope usable only in FROM lines. Once you're inside a stage, you must re-declare `ARG TAG` to use its value in RUN/ENV, because each FROM resets the ARG scope. This trips people up constantly.

followups.q: If ARG isn't present at runtime, how can a stale --build-arg still cause a surprise rebuild or value?
followups.a: An ARG's value is part of the cache key for instructions after its declaration, so changing `--build-arg` busts cache from that point down even though the value never reaches runtime. And if you forget to redeclare an ARG in a later stage, it silently falls back to its default — leading to a mismatch between what you passed and what the stage used.

followups.q: Which ARGs does BuildKit predefine, and why does that matter for proxies?
followups.a: BuildKit predefines proxy ARGs like `HTTP_PROXY`, `HTTPS_PROXY`, `NO_PROXY` (and lowercase): if you pass them as build args they're consumed by RUN commands but, by design, excluded from `docker history` and don't bust cache, so proxy settings don't leak into the image. That's why proxies "just work" in builds without polluting the final image config.

q: What is BuildKit and what does a cache mount (RUN --mount=type=cache) buy you?
a: BuildKit is the default build engine: it builds a graph of the Dockerfile so independent stages run in parallel, skips stages whose output is unused, and adds mount types for caches and secrets. A cache mount persists a directory (a package or compiler cache like ~/.cache/pip or /root/.m2) across builds without baking it into a layer, so a dependency install reuses downloads even when the layer itself is invalidated. The mounted directory is not part of the final image, which keeps it small. For shared caches like apt, add sharing=locked to serialize concurrent builds.
fs: A cache mount persists a build-only directory across builds and is never written into the final image layers.
fj: A cache mount just bakes the package cache into a layer so the next build is faster.

code.label: reusable pip cache, not in image
lines: # reused next build, not in the image

followups.q: What are the three sharing modes for a cache mount and when do you pick each?
followups.a: `shared` (default) lets concurrent builds write the same cache simultaneously — fine for tools that handle concurrency. `locked` makes a second build wait for the first to release the mount — required for apt and other non-concurrency-safe caches. `private` gives each concurrent build its own cache copy. Pick `locked` for apt, `shared` for most language package managers.

followups.q: How does a cache mount differ from layer caching, and can a cache mount be lost?
followups.a: Layer caching reuses whole built layers keyed on instruction inputs; a cache mount is a persistent scratch directory that survives even when the layer is rebuilt. It's a build-host artifact, not part of the image, so it's not exported with the image and can be pruned (`docker builder prune`) or absent on a fresh CI runner — making it a speedup, never a correctness dependency.

followups.q: On ephemeral CI runners the cache mount is empty each run. How do you still benefit?
followups.a: Cache mounts are local to the builder, so on fresh runners they start cold. To persist across runs you combine them with exported layer cache (`--cache-to/--cache-from type=registry|gha`) for the install layers, or use a persistent/remote BuildKit builder. Some CI setups also restore the cache-mount directory from the CI's own cache store keyed on the lockfile.

q: How do you scan a container image for vulnerabilities, and where does scanning fit in a pipeline?
a: A scanner (Trivy, Grype, docker scout, Snyk) reads the image's package metadata and layers, builds an SBOM of installed OS and language dependencies, and matches them against CVE feeds to report known vulnerabilities by severity. Run it in CI on every build and fail the pipeline above a chosen severity threshold so vulnerable images never reach a registry. Scanning is a snapshot in time, so also re-scan images already in the registry as new CVEs are published. The usual fixes are bumping the base image, patching packages, or switching to a slimmer base with fewer packages to exploit.
fs: Scanners match the image's SBOM against CVE feeds; gate CI on severity and re-scan stored images as new CVEs land.
fj: If the build succeeds the image is safe; scanning is only needed if something looks broken.

followups.q: A scan reports a critical CVE in your base OS that has no fix yet. What are your options?
followups.a: Triage reachability — if your app never invokes the vulnerable code path the risk may be low; document an accepted-risk/ignore entry (e.g. a `.trivyignore` with justification and expiry). Otherwise switch to a slimmer base (distroless/-slim) that doesn't ship that package, pin to a patched upstream, or rebuild when a fix lands. Don't blanket-suppress; track and re-review.

followups.q: Why is a distroless or minimal base often a faster fix for scan findings than patching?
followups.a: Most CVEs are in OS packages you don't actually use (shells, package managers, libc utilities). A distroless/scratch base ships almost none of them, so the vulnerability count drops to near-zero without you patching anything — fewer packages literally means fewer matchable CVEs and a smaller attack surface.

followups.q: What is an SBOM and why generate one separately from scanning?
followups.a: An SBOM (e.g. SPDX or CycloneDX) is a machine-readable inventory of every component and version in the image. You attach it to the image (as an attestation) so consumers and future scanners can re-assess against new CVE feeds without rebuilding — supply-chain provenance. `docker buildx` can emit SBOM and provenance attestations at build time for exactly this.

q: Why pin an image by digest instead of a tag, and what is the tradeoff?
a: A tag like :latest or :1.2 is a mutable pointer — it can be re-pushed to a different image, accidentally by CI or maliciously by someone with registry access — so two pulls of the same tag can give different bytes. A digest (image@sha256:...) is the content-addressed hash of the image, so pinning to it guarantees you always run the exact bits you tested, which is why digest pinning is a supply-chain control. The tradeoff is that digests are opaque and don't auto-update, so you pair a readable tag with the digest (image:1.2@sha256:...) and let a bot like Dependabot bump the pin deliberately.
fs: Tags are mutable pointers; a digest is the immutable content hash, so pin by digest for reproducible, tamper-evident pulls.
fj: Pinning to :1.2 is already immutable, so a digest adds nothing.

code.label: tag + digest pin
lines: # re-push of the tag can't change

followups.q: You pin a multi-arch image by digest. Does the digest point at one architecture's bits?
followups.a: For a multi-arch image the digest usually points at the manifest list (image index), which itself references per-arch manifests by their own digests. So pinning the index digest still resolves to the right arch at pull time via platform matching — you're not locked to one architecture, you're locked to that exact set of arch images.

followups.q: Does digest pinning alone prove the image came from who you think? If not, what does?
followups.a: No — a digest proves immutability and integrity (you got those exact bytes), but not authenticity of the publisher. For provenance you add signing: `cosign`/Sigstore signatures and attestations, verified at admission (e.g. policy controllers, Docker Content Trust historically). Digest + signature together give you tamper-evidence and trusted origin.

followups.q: How do teams keep digest pins from going stale and missing security patches?
followups.a: Automate the bump: Dependabot/Renovate watch the readable tag, resolve its new digest, and open a PR updating `image:1.2@sha256:...`. CI re-tests the new bits before merge. This keeps the reproducibility benefit of pinning while still flowing patched bases in on a reviewed cadence rather than silently.

q: Two builds from the same source produce different image digests. Why, and how do you get reproducible builds?
a: Most non-determinism comes from inputs that change between builds: timestamps written into layers, unpinned base tags or apt/pip versions that float, network-fetched files, and embedded build dates. To get bit-for-bit reproducibility, pin the base by digest and pin package versions, avoid unpinned downloads, and set SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH so BuildKit rewrites file and image timestamps to a fixed value. Reproducibility matters because it lets you verify an image was actually built from a given commit and underpins build provenance/attestations.
fs: Pin bases by digest, pin package versions, and set SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH so floating versions and timestamps stop perturbing the digest.
fj: Builds from the same Dockerfile always produce the same digest, so reproducibility is automatic.

followups.q: How exactly does SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH affect the build, and what doesn't it cover?
followups.a: BuildKit reads `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH` (a Unix timestamp) and rewrites layer file mtimes and the image's created timestamp to that fixed value, removing the most common source of digest drift. It doesn't fix non-determinism inside your build (e.g. a compiler embedding a build path or random ordering); those need their own reproducibility flags.

followups.q: Why can two builds still differ even after pinning bases and setting SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH?
followups.a: Build-internal nondeterminism: compilers embedding absolute paths or `__DATE__`, archives written in filesystem-iteration order, randomized symbol/hash ordering, or `pip`/`npm` resolving floating transitive deps. Fixes include path-remapping flags (`-ffile-prefix-map`), sorting tar entries, fixing `PYTHONHASHSEED`, and fully locked dependency trees.

followups.q: How does reproducibility connect to SLSA / build provenance in practice?
followups.a: Provenance attestations (e.g. SLSA, emitted by `docker buildx --provenance`) record the source commit, builder, and inputs. Reproducibility lets an independent verifier rebuild from that commit and confirm they get the same digest the attestation claims — closing the loop so a signed provenance statement is actually checkable, not just asserted.

q: What is an OCI image and a registry, and what actually happens on docker push/pull?
a: An OCI image is a standardized package: a manifest that lists a config blob (entrypoint, env, layer order) plus the layer blobs, all content-addressed by digest — the open spec that lets non-Docker tools build and run the same images. A registry (Docker Hub, ECR, GHCR) is a content-addressable store of those blobs keyed by digest, with tags as human-friendly pointers. push uploads only the layer blobs the registry doesn't already have, then the manifest; pull resolves the tag to a manifest digest and downloads the missing layers. Because layers are shared by digest, common bases are stored and transferred once.
fs: A registry stores content-addressed OCI blobs; push/pull move only the missing layers and a tag is just a pointer to a manifest digest.
fj: A registry stores whole images as single files, and each push uploads the entire image again.

followups.q: How does a single tag serve both an ARM and an x86 host transparently?
followups.a: The tag points at an image index (manifest list) that references per-platform manifests, each tagged with `os/architecture`. On pull, the client sends its platform and the registry/runtime selects the matching manifest and its layers. So `docker pull app:1.0` on an M-series Mac and an x86 server resolve the same tag to different underlying images.

followups.q: During a push, how does the registry know which layers it can skip uploading?
followups.a: The client first issues HEAD/mount requests for each layer blob by digest; if the registry already has that content-addressed blob (often from another image sharing the base) it returns exists and the client skips the upload, sometimes via cross-repo blob mount. Only genuinely new blobs and the manifest are transferred — that's the dedup that makes pushes of shared bases cheap.

followups.q: Beyond images, what else can an OCI registry store these days?
followups.a: The OCI distribution spec now carries arbitrary artifacts via the referrers API: Helm charts, SBOMs, signatures (cosign), provenance attestations, and even AI models are pushed as OCI artifacts linked to an image by digest. So a registry is increasingly a general content-addressable artifact store, with attestations discoverable as referrers of the image they describe.

q: Why are Docker containers fundamentally Linux-native, and what happens when you run Docker on macOS or Windows?
a: Docker containers share the host Linux kernel — they are isolated processes using Linux namespaces and cgroups, not full VMs with their own OS. There is no Windows or macOS equivalent of the container primitives, so Docker Desktop ships a lightweight Linux VM (HyperKit on Mac, Hyper-V/WSL2 on Windows) to provide the Linux kernel. Every container you run on those platforms actually runs inside that Linux guest, which is why host networking, direct device access, and performance characteristics differ from Linux native.
fs: Containers are Linux kernel features; macOS/Windows run a Linux VM under Docker Desktop — there is no native container runtime on those OSes.
fj: Docker containers are cross-platform and use the native macOS or Windows kernel when run there.

followups.q: Why does `--network host` behave differently on Docker Desktop versus Linux?
followups.a: On Linux, `--network host` removes the container's network namespace so it shares the host's interfaces directly. On Docker Desktop, the 'host' is the Linux VM, not your Mac or Windows machine, so the container joins the VM's network stack — not your laptop's. Docker Desktop added experimental support to bridge this, but truly native host-stack sharing is a Linux-only property.

followups.q: What are the practical latency consequences of the Linux VM layer on Docker Desktop for I/O-heavy workloads?
followups.a: Bind mounts on Docker Desktop cross a virtio/9P or gRPC-Fuse filesystem boundary between macOS and the Linux VM, adding significant latency for file-intensive operations like `npm install` or live code reloading. Mitigations include using named volumes (data stays inside the Linux VM), the VirtioFS driver (faster on Apple Silicon), or delegating heavy I/O to the container side entirely.

q: How do Linux namespaces isolate containers, and which namespace types does Docker use?
a: Linux namespaces partition global kernel resources so each container sees its own private view. Docker uses six: pid (each container has its own process tree with PID 1), net (private network interfaces and routing table), mnt (private filesystem mount tree, basis for overlayfs), uts (private hostname), ipc (private System V IPC and POSIX message queues), and user (optional — maps in-container UIDs to host UIDs). The kernel does not copy these resources; each namespace is a view that shares the same underlying kernel, which is why container startup is milliseconds rather than the seconds a full VM needs.
fs: Docker uses pid/net/mnt/uts/ipc/user Linux namespaces — each is a lightweight view of a kernel resource, not a copy.
fj: Docker uses a single hypervisor namespace that wraps the container in a VM.

followups.q: What does sharing the Linux kernel mean for container security compared to a VM?
followups.a: All containers on a host share one Linux kernel, so a kernel-level exploit can break namespace isolation and affect all tenants — something a hypervisor boundary prevents. This is why multi-tenant Kubernetes clusters often add a second isolation layer (gVisor, Kata Containers, or strict seccomp/AppArmor) and why node separation between trust domains is a standard hardening control.

followups.q: When would you explicitly use the pid namespace of another container, and what does that give you?
followups.a: `docker run --pid container:<id>` places the new container in another container's pid namespace, so both see the same process tree. This is how ephemeral debug containers work: the debug container can `strace`, inspect `/proc`, or send signals to processes in the target container's tree — without being part of its image or filesystem.

q: Explain Linux cgroups and how Docker uses them to enforce resource limits on containers.
a: Control groups (cgroups) are a Linux kernel mechanism that organizes processes into a hierarchy and applies resource accounting and limits per group. Docker creates a cgroup per container and wires container flags to cgroup controllers: `--memory` sets the memory.max limit, `--cpus` maps to cpu.weight/cpu.max in cgroups v2, and `--blkio-weight` throttles block I/O. When a container exceeds its memory limit the Linux OOM killer terminates processes in that cgroup — that is the exit code 137 signal. Docker switched to cgroups v2 as default in recent releases; v2 unifies the hierarchy and adds pressure-stall info for better accounting.
fs: Docker maps --memory/--cpus flags directly to Linux cgroup limits; OOM kills inside the container are the kernel enforcing those limits.
fj: Docker limits CPU and memory with its own scheduler independent of the Linux kernel.

followups.q: What is the difference between `--memory` and `--memory-swap`, and why set them equal?
followups.a: `--memory` caps RAM usage; `--memory-swap` caps RAM + swap together. Setting them equal disables swap for that container — important for latency-sensitive services where swap thrashing is worse than an OOM kill, and for accurate JVM heap sizing so the JVM doesn't overcommit into invisible swap.

followups.q: How do cgroups v1 and v2 differ in how Docker configures CPU limits?
followups.a: In cgroups v1 Docker set `cpu.shares` (relative weight) and `cpu.cfs_period_us`/`cpu.cfs_quota_us` for hard throttle. In cgroups v2 those are replaced by `cpu.weight` (relative) and `cpu.max` (quota/period unified). Docker uses the right interface based on the host Linux kernel version; v2 also adds per-cgroup pressure-stall indicators so tooling can detect CPU/memory pressure before OOM.

q: How does the Linux overlayfs storage driver work for Docker, and what is copy-on-write?
a: overlayfs is a Linux union filesystem that merges multiple directory trees into a single coherent view. Docker's overlay2 driver stacks read-only image layers as `lowerdir` entries and adds one writable `upperdir` per container. Reads serve the file from the highest layer that contains it. A write to a file that lives only in a lowerdir triggers copy-on-write (CoW): the Linux kernel copies the full file into upperdir, then the process modifies that copy — the original lowerdir byte is untouched. This is why a container that writes a single byte to a 100 MB binary amplifies I/O: the whole file is copied up. CoW is also why the first write to a cold container is slower than subsequent writes.
fs: overlayfs CoW copies the entire file from lowerdir to upperdir on first write — a 100 MB binary write amplifies to a 100 MB read+write.
fj: overlayfs writes only the changed bytes, so copy-on-write has no I/O overhead.

code.label: overlayfs mount anatomy
lines: # Linux kernel merges: rw shadows L2 shadows L1 shadows L0

followups.q: When is it worth switching away from overlay2, and to what?
followups.a: overlay2 is the right default for most Linux workloads. For database containers that do heavy random writes to large files, the CoW overhead per file is significant; those workloads are better served with a named volume on the host filesystem (ext4/xfs directly) bypassing overlay2 entirely. For systems where overlay2 requires a kernel older than 4.0 (rare in 2026), devicemapper was the legacy fallback.

followups.q: What is the `workdir` in an overlayfs mount, and what happens if it's on a different filesystem?
followups.a: `workdir` is a temporary staging directory the Linux kernel uses for atomic copy-up operations — files are written there first then moved atomically to `upperdir`. It must be on the same filesystem as `upperdir` because the kernel uses rename(2) which doesn't cross filesystem boundaries. Docker's overlay2 driver enforces this; putting upperdir on a different fs causes mount failures.

q: What are Linux capabilities, and how does Docker use --cap-drop and --cap-add to implement least-privilege containers?
a: Linux capabilities split traditional root privilege into discrete units: CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE binds privileged ports, CAP_SYS_PTRACE allows process tracing, CAP_CHOWN changes file ownership, and so on. Docker starts containers with a default set of about 14 capabilities — enough for most apps but not full root. `--cap-drop ALL` removes everything, then `--cap-add` re-grants only what the app needs. This principle of least privilege means a compromised container has far fewer kernel attack vectors than a fully privileged one, limiting blast radius even if namespace isolation is bypassed.
fs: Docker grants ~14 capabilities by default; --cap-drop ALL then --cap-add <specific> applies least privilege so a breach has fewer kernel vectors.
fj: If a container runs as non-root it has no capabilities, so --cap-drop is redundant.

code.label: drop all, add only what's needed

followups.q: What does --security-opt no-new-privileges do beyond cap-drop?
followups.a: It sets the Linux `PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS` flag on the container's init process, preventing any child process from gaining capabilities via setuid binaries or file capabilities — even if a setuid root binary is present in the image. This stops privilege escalation attacks that work by exec-ing a setuid file rather than a kernel exploit.

followups.q: Which default Docker capability is most commonly the source of container escapes, and why?
followups.a: CAP_SYS_ADMIN is the broadest and most dangerous capability — it covers dozens of kernel operations including mounting filesystems, manipulating namespaces, and more. It's sometimes added for privileged use cases but is essentially partial root. CAP_NET_ADMIN and CAP_SYS_PTRACE are also frequently misused. Auditing Kubernetes securityContext and Docker run flags for these is a standard security review step.

q: What is seccomp in the context of Linux containers, and how does Docker's default seccomp profile protect you?
a: seccomp (secure computing mode) is a Linux kernel feature that filters which system calls a process is allowed to invoke, and kills or returns EPERM for blocked ones. Docker ships a default seccomp profile (a JSON allowlist) that permits the ~300 syscalls a typical container needs and blocks dangerous ones like `kexec_load`, `open_by_handle_at`, `perf_event_open`, and `bpf`. This is a defense-in-depth layer: even if a container escapes namespace isolation, a blocked syscall prevents many kernel exploits. You can supply a custom profile with `--security-opt seccomp=profile.json` or disable it with `seccomp=unconfined` for debugging.
fs: Docker's default Linux seccomp profile blocks ~50 dangerous syscalls; it's defense-in-depth — a complement to, not a replacement for, namespace isolation.
fj: seccomp is only needed for containers that run privileged mode; regular containers don't use it.

followups.q: How does seccomp interact with capabilities — are they redundant?
followups.a: They operate at different kernel layers and are complementary. Capabilities gate coarse privilege tokens (e.g. CAP_NET_ADMIN). seccomp filters at the syscall level regardless of capability — even a process with CAP_SYS_ADMIN is blocked from a seccomp-denied syscall. Both are needed: capabilities prevent privilege escalation, seccomp limits the exploitable kernel surface once a container has certain capabilities.

followups.q: A container crashes with SIGSYS instead of EPERM after tightening seccomp. Why and how do you debug it?
followups.a: SIGSYS means the process was killed by a seccomp KILL action (not just ERRNO). To find the blocked call, switch the action to ERRNO temporarily and watch `strace` or `auditd` logs for EPERM on unexpected syscalls. Alternatively run with `seccomp=unconfined` to confirm the profile is the cause, then narrow by adding suspect syscalls to the allowlist one at a time.

q: What is rootless Docker, and how does it use Linux user namespaces to run without host root?
a: Rootless Docker runs both the daemon and all containers as an unprivileged Linux user with no SUID binaries. It uses Linux user namespace remapping to give the container the illusion of being root (UID 0 inside) while mapping that to an unprivileged UID on the host (e.g. UID 100000+). Network namespaces use `slirp4netns` (a userspace TCP/IP stack) instead of kernel bridging since creating veth pairs requires privilege. This eliminates the host-root daemon attack surface and is a strong defense-in-depth control, at the cost of some performance overhead and feature limitations (no privileged port binding, some drivers unavailable).
fs: Rootless Docker maps container UID 0 to an unprivileged host UID via Linux user namespaces — the daemon itself needs no host root.
fj: Rootless Docker is just running the container with a non-root USER; the daemon still runs as root.

followups.q: What Linux kernel version and /etc/subuid configuration are required for rootless Docker?
followups.a: Rootless Docker needs Linux kernel 5.11+ for cgroups v2 delegation without root, though basic rootless works from ~4.18 with user namespaces enabled. `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid` must grant the user a range of at least 65536 subordinate IDs (e.g. `alice:100000:65536`). The `newuidmap`/`newgidmap` setuid helpers (from shadow-utils) perform the mapping without full root.

followups.q: What are the main functional limitations of rootless Docker compared to the rootful daemon?
followups.a: Rootless can't bind ports below 1024 (no CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE), doesn't support overlay2 on older kernels without kernel patches, can't use host networking (slirp4netns is userspace-only and slower), and some storage drivers aren't available. Privileged containers (`--privileged`) still require the rootful daemon. For most stateless services these are non-issues, but databases and network-intensive workloads may need the rootful path.

q: How does Docker's `--privileged` flag relate to Linux kernel access, and why is it dangerous in production?
a: `--privileged` grants the container all Linux capabilities, disables seccomp filtering, disables AppArmor/SELinux confinement, and mounts the host's /dev tree inside the container. In effect the container has near-full access to the Linux kernel and host filesystem — a privileged container can load kernel modules, mount arbitrary filesystems (including the host's root), modify networking, and escape to the host trivially with a few commands. It is used legitimately for Docker-in-Docker CI and some low-level sysadmin tools, but in production it is almost always unnecessary and represents a full security boundary collapse.
fs: --privileged drops all Linux security controls: all caps granted, seccomp off, AppArmor off, host /dev exposed — a container escape is trivial.
fj: --privileged just gives the container root, same as running the process as root without extra kernel access.

followups.q: What is the minimal alternative to --privileged for Docker-in-Docker?
followups.a: Use the `docker:dind` image with only `--cap-add SYS_ADMIN` and a mounted tmpfs for `/var/lib/docker`, or better, use Docker socket forwarding (`-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock`) so the inner Docker client talks to the host daemon — no privilege needed inside the CI container. Kaniko and Buildah offer fully rootless image builds without needing the Linux kernel socket at all.

followups.q: How would you detect --privileged containers running in a production cluster?
followups.a: In Kubernetes, audit `securityContext.privileged: true` via admission controllers (OPA/Gatekeeper, Kyverno) and enforce a policy that denies it except in approved namespaces. For Docker hosts, `docker inspect --format '{{.HostConfig.Privileged}}' $(docker ps -q)` lists privileged containers. Falco can alert at runtime when a container gains an unusual set of Linux capabilities consistent with privileged mode.

q: What Linux kernel feature underlies Docker image layer deduplication, and how does overlay2 manage the on-disk layer graph?
a: Docker's overlay2 driver exploits the Linux overlayfs kernel module, which supports stacking up to 128 lowerdir layers in a single mount. On disk, each image layer is stored as a plain directory under `/var/lib/docker/overlay2/<sha>/diff`; Docker builds a `link`/`lower` chain where each layer's `lower` file lists the chain of lower layer IDs. When a container starts, the daemon assembles the full lowerdir list from root to the top image layer, adds a container-specific `upperdir` and `workdir`, and issues one `mount -t overlay` call. The Linux kernel does the rest in-kernel with no data copying. Layers with identical content-addressed digests are stored once and shared across images, which is why `docker pull` only downloads layers a host doesn't already have.
fs: overlay2 uses Linux overlayfs: each image layer is a diff directory; the kernel merges them all in one mount with no copying at startup.
fj: Docker copies each layer into a merged directory on disk when a container starts.

code.label: inspect overlay2 mount
lines: # LowerDir is colon-delimited chain of image layers

followups.q: What is the practical overlay2 layer limit, and how does a deep multi-stage build hit it?
followups.a: The Linux overlayfs kernel module supports at most 128 lowerdir entries in a single mount; that translates to ~128 image layers. A long chain of FROM stages or hundreds of RUN instructions can approach this limit, causing the build or container start to fail with 'too many levels of symbolic links'. The fix is collapsing layers with multi-stage builds, combining RUN chains, or using `--squash`.

followups.q: How does `docker system prune` interact with the overlay2 layer store?
followups.a: `docker system prune` removes stopped containers (their upperdir) and dangling image layers (diff directories unreferenced by any image tag). It doesn't touch layers that any tagged image or running container references. `--volumes` also prunes named volumes. The freed space is only visible after the overlay2 directories are deleted; in-use layers are reference-counted by the daemon and won't be removed while a container is running.

q: How do you govern base-image selection across hundreds of microservices so the platform isn't running dozens of different base-image lineages?
a: Establish a golden-image catalog: a small set of org-curated base images (distroless, slim, language-specific) built from pinned upstream digests, vulnerability-scanned, and published to an internal registry under a controlled namespace. Enforce catalog membership at admission: a Kyverno or OPA/Gatekeeper policy rejects pods whose FROM doesn't match an approved base pattern, and CI fails builds that don't extend a catalog image. A bot (Renovate or internal tooling) opens PRs to bump catalog bases when the upstream patches; human review flows through a lightweight RFC — every service inherits the fix automatically on next build. Track lineage in image attestations so you can answer 'how many services are on python:3.12 right now' in seconds. The result: vulnerability patches reach all services without per-team action, and the audit trail is machines-readable.
